text: {"claims":[{"statement":"Chicken eggs are an affordable source of protein and other nutrients.","claimType":"definitional","quote":"Chicken eggs are an affordable source of protein and other price.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"definitional"},{"statement":"Chicken eggs are naturally high in cholesterol.","claimType":"definitional","quote":"They're also naturally high in cholesterol.","entailment":"The quoted sentence directly asserts that chicken eggs contain a high amount of cholesterol naturally.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"definitional"},{"statement":"The cholesterol in eggs does not seem to raise cholesterol levels the way some other foods do.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"But the cholesterol in eggs doesn't seem to raise cholesterol levels the way some other foods, such as those high in trans fats and saturated fats, do.","entailment":"The quote compares the effect of egg cholesterol to that of other foods, indicating that egg cholesterol appears not to increase blood cholesterol levels as strongly as other foods.","population":"","intervention":"cholesterol from eggs","comparator":"cholesterol from trans‑fat‑rich or saturated‑fat‑rich foods","outcome":"cholesterol levels","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Some studies have found a link between eating eggs and heart disease.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Although some studies have found a link between eating eggs and heart disease, ...","entailment":"The quoted phrase reports that research has observed an association between egg consumption and heart disease.","population":"","intervention":"eating eggs","comparator":"","outcome":"heart disease","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Foods commonly eaten with eggs might do more to boost heart disease risk than eggs do.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"The foods people typically eat with eggs, such as bacon, sausage and ham, might do more to boost heart disease risk than eggs do.","entailment":"The quote suggests that accompanying foods may have a greater impact on heart disease risk than the eggs themselves.","population":"","intervention":"bacon, sausage, ham (foods eaten with eggs)","comparator":"eggs alone","outcome":"heart disease risk","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"The way eggs and other foods are cooked might play more of a role in increased heart disease risk than eggs themselves do.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Plus, the way eggs and other foods are cooked, especially if fried in oil or butter, might play more of a role in the increased risk of heart disease than eggs themselves do.","entailment":"The quoted sentence attributes a larger contribution to cooking method than to the eggs themselves for heart disease risk.","population":"","intervention":"fried eggs or other foods cooked in oil or butter","comparator":"eggs not fried / other cooking methods","outcome":"increased heart disease risk","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Most healthy people can eat up to seven eggs a week without increasing their risk of heart disease.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Most healthy people can eat up to seven eggs a week without increasing their risk of heart disease.","entailment":"The quote reports that, for healthy individuals, consuming up to seven eggs weekly does not raise heart disease risk.","population":"healthy people","intervention":"up to seven eggs per week","comparator":"","outcome":"risk of heart disease","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"up to seven eggs a week","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Eating up to seven eggs a week might help prevent certain types of stroke.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Some studies have shown that this level of egg consumption might even help prevent certain types of stroke ...","entailment":"The quoted phrase indicates that research suggests a possible protective effect of that egg consumption level against some strokes.","population":"","intervention":"up to seven eggs per week","comparator":"","outcome":"prevention of certain types of stroke","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"this level of egg consumption","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Eating up to seven eggs a week might help prevent macular degeneration.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"...and a serious eye condition called macular degeneration that can lead to blindness.","entailment":"The same sentence linking egg consumption to stroke also mentions a possible benefit for macular degeneration, implying a protective association.","population":"","intervention":"up to seven eggs per week","comparator":"","outcome":"prevention of macular degeneration","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"this level of egg consumption","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"For people with diabetes, eating seven eggs a week increases heart disease risk.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"But if you have diabetes, some research suggests that eating seven eggs a week increases heart disease risk.","entailment":"The quote reports that, among diabetics, consuming seven eggs weekly is associated with higher heart disease risk.","population":"people with diabetes","intervention":"seven eggs per week","comparator":"","outcome":"heart disease risk","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"seven eggs a week","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Other research failed to find a connection between seven eggs a week and heart disease risk in diabetics.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"However, other research failed to find the same connection.","entailment":"The quoted sentence states that some studies did not observe the previously mentioned association.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Eating eggs might increase the risk of developing diabetes.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Still other research suggests that eating eggs might increase the risk of developing diabetes in the first place.","entailment":"The quote indicates a possible association between egg consumption and a higher chance of later developing diabetes.","population":"","intervention":"eating eggs","comparator":"","outcome":"risk of developing diabetes","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Health experts suggest limiting dietary cholesterol to under 300 mg per day.","claimType":"value","quote":"Health experts now suggest eating as little dietary cholesterol as you can, aiming to keep intake under 300 milligrams (mg) a day.","entailment":"The quoted recommendation directly advises keeping cholesterol intake below 300 mg daily.","population":"","intervention":"dietary cholesterol intake","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"under 300 mg a day","modality":"normative"},{"statement":"One large egg contains about 186 mg of cholesterol, all in the yolk.","claimType":"definitional","quote":"One large egg has about 186 mg of cholesterol, all of which is found in the yolk.","entailment":"The quote provides the factual amount of cholesterol in a large egg and locates it in the yolk.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"about 186 mg per large egg","modality":"definitional"},{"statement":"If a diet contains little other cholesterol, eating up to an egg a day might be acceptable.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"If your diet contains little other cholesterol, according to some studies, eating up to an egg a day might be an OK choice.","entailment":"The quote suggests that, when other cholesterol sources are minimal, consuming one egg daily may be permissible based on some research.","population":"","intervention":"up to one egg per day","comparator":"","outcome":"acceptability of egg consumption","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"up to an egg a day","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Egg whites contain no cholesterol but still contain protein.","claimType":"definitional","quote":"Egg whites contain no cholesterol but still contain protein.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"definitional"}]}
